title: The Torturer, by Peter Saxon - Book Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8hbiWsIlhQ0

October review number two, and the exact opposite of the Lafferty: where The Devil is Dead was exquisite prose with no plot-pull, Peter Saxon's The Torturer (1967) is 'competently written — and that might be generous' but genuinely entertaining. Daniel (@dungeondive) reminds us 'Peter Saxon' never existed: it was a house pseudonym shared by a stable of writers churning out occult adventure paperbacks. His big claim is a fun one: this reads like a slasher novel written four years before Bava's Bay of Blood supposedly birthed the genre. A film crew scouts a Spanish castle — the Castello della Morte — accidentally wakes its resurrected torturer-count, and gets hunted down one by one amid death-trap-dungeon corridors. Daniel is genuinely surprised it never became an Italian horror film and half-suspects it inspired a few (House by the Cemetery, Burial Ground). The problems are equally period-accurate: characters he couldn't care less about and misogyny straight from the giallo playbook — lecherous men, conniving women, one character slurred dozens of times in 150 pages. Still, the atmosphere and the castle's traps carried him through, and he's already ordered Satan's Child. The review ends with a resolution: two mediocre books in a row means the next read must be a sure thing.

----

A 1967 paperback hitting every slasher beat years before the movies — does that make The Torturer ahead of its time, or proof the slasher formula was always older than cinema?
